sections.heading: What's an array?
sections.body: An **array** is a row of numbered boxes. Each box holds one value. Each box has a position — called an **index** — starting from `0`, not `1`.

sections.title: Houses on a street
sections.body: Picture a short street where every house is numbered: 0, 1, 2, 3. Every house has a person inside. If I say 'go to house 2', you know exactly where to walk. That's an array — instant access by position.

sections.caption: An array of 4 numbers. Notice indices start at 0.

sections.code:
const nums = [10, 20, 30, 40];

console.log(nums[0]);       // 10  — first element
console.log(nums[2]);       // 30  — third element
console.log(nums.length);   // 4   — how many elements

nums[1] = 99;               // change a slot
console.log(nums);          // [10, 99, 30, 40]

sections.caption: Try it: add, remove, and search elements live.

sections.heading: What arrays are good at
sections.body:
Arrays are **incredibly fast** at one thing: jumping to a position. Getting `arr[10000]` takes the same time as getting `arr[0]`. That's O(1).

They're **slow** at one thing: inserting or removing in the middle. Everything after the change has to shift over. That's O(n).

questions.question: Given `const arr = [5, 10, 15];`, what does `arr[1]` give?
questions.explanation: Indices start at 0, so arr[1] is the SECOND element — 10.

questions.question: What gets logged?
questions.explanation: Setting index 5 stretches the array — its length becomes 6 (with empty slots in between).

questions.question: What's the time complexity of arr[i]?
questions.explanation: Array indexing is constant time — the computer goes directly to that memory slot.
